data.title: Forgent Power Solutions: Powering On Post Its Public Offering
data.description:
When Forgent Power Solutions (FPS) went public early in February, I wondered if shares had the power. Currently, shares have been trading largely flattish in a $26-$37 price range, with shares exchanging hands around the $30 mark.

Compared to the time of the offering two months ago, my regard for the business has improved a great deal, in between accelerating growth, margin gains, strong guidance, a strong order intake, and remarks about long-term growth and capacity.

This is highly encouraging in my view, amidst a relatively stable share price while market conditions at large are difficult. This makes me quite upbeat here. My regard for the business has increased a great deal, and dips from here certainly look compelling enough to buy into dips.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Recent News

In the final days of March, Forgent sold a total of some 34.5 million shares at $29.50 per share. Nearly 24 million shares were sold by selling shareholders, with the company offering 10.8 million shares, including the overallotment option here.

The offering came relatively soon after the offering, and Forgent reported second-quarter results in the middle of the month. That was the first public report of Forgent as a publicly traded business.

These numbers look quite solid at face value, with reported sales up 69% to $296 million, driven by data center and grid customers. GAAP operating profits of $20 million were up minimally on the year before, suggesting real deleverage in percentage terms. Quarterly revenues rose a bit from a $283 million number for the first quarter, yet GAAP operating margins have come in quite a bit from a $31 million number in the first quarter.

With interest expenses largely coming in around levels of operating profits, the company posted largely break-even results. Those are the GAAP results, with adjusted earnings for the quarter seen around $35 million. Much of the reconciliation seems fair, but on the other hand, earnings are quite aggressive in the number of adjustments being made.

Of course, the results were applicable for the period that ended on the 31st of December, so ahead of the public offering taking place.

Note that the mixed results, that of sequential growth in sales and pressure on margins, contrast with momentum seen elsewhere in the business. Quarterly bookings of $762 million come in at more than 2.5 times reported sales! The backlog rose by 100% on an annual basis to $1.5 billion, indicating that topline growth might accelerate and likely margin gains can be delivered upon going forward.

For the year, the company sees full-year sales between $1.275 billion and $1.325 billion, with adjusted EBITDA seen between $300 and $310 million. Based on the performance year to date, the implied guidance for the remaining two quarters of the year suggests quarterly revenues of $360 million and adjusted EBITDA of $90 million. That looks rather strong, with the second quarter adjusted EBITDA reported at $60 million.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

The company ended the second quarter with a net debt load of $477 million, as the company has been investing quite a bit in its new facilities. The company sees this carrying a capacity to support $5 billion in annual revenues, with maintenance capital spending seen dropping to just about 1 percent of sales. Following the IPO at $27 per share, the company has seen solid demand for the manufacturing of various electrical distribution equipment, these being "engineered to order" applications. Typical applications are seen in data center power distribution, utility substations, and various energy-intensive manufacturing applications. In fact, data centers make up over 40% of sales in 2025, with grid customers making up nearly another quarter of sales. Needless to say, such solutions are in great demand, as is evident in the revenue (growth) numbers, but moreover, the backlog numbers, which show even more momentum here. For the valuation part, it should be seen that private equity firm Neos has been active with M&A in the "construction" of Forgent, having built up the business through a series of acquisitions here.

At the time of the offering, I believed that there were some 304 million shares outstanding. Shares sold by selling shareholders are already included in this number, as Forgent itself has sold nearly 11 million shares recently, for a 315 million pro forma share count. In between $432 million in gross proceeds from the IPO (excluding the overallotment option), the existing net debt load, and recent equity issuance, I see pro forma net cash holdings around $300 million.

Trading at $30, the market value stands at about $9.5 billion, for a $9.2 billion enterprise valuation.

On Fire

Based on the outlook for the second half of the year, revenues trend around $1.44 billion, valuing the business at just over 6 times sales. Adjusted EBITDA trends around $360 million, for a 25 times multiple on that front. Both of these are quite solid numbers, much stronger than I anticipated in February, certainly as it was not clear which role the M&A played in the pace of growth. In the absence of recent M&A here, growth seems to be accelerating, which is looking good.

Moreover, with capital spending seen as minimal going forward, real earnings power is seen here, in between a net unleveraged balance sheet. That suggests that earnings could come in close to around a dollar per share. All this is much stronger than I believed in February, all of which is quite comforting with earnings power rapidly providing support to the valuation here.

Moreover, earnings are near-term depressed, with revenue numbers trending softer compared to booking numbers, which trend closer to $3 billion based on the number reported this quarter. Furthermore, the company has a large runway for growth, with its current capacity seen as facilitating $5 billion in sales.

Despite the current rapid growth trajectory, the company is only producing at around 30% of capacity. This is very encouraging; certainly, as the company needs to absorb the extra capacity of the new factory and is hiring ahead of a ramp-up in sales, leading to suboptimal near-term margins.

Given all this, much more potential remains in the work. I have become much more appealed to Forgent here, even as the company needs to spend a remaining $73 million in terms of capital spending to complete the factory here.

Strong momentum is very welcome, and my regard for the business has improved a great deal compared to the time of the offering, given the accelerating growth, convincing outlook, strong backlog, and positive remarks about capacity and future growth.

data.title: Invesco American Franchise Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The market experienced a period of digestion

Growth equities underperformed value during the quarter after outperforming since the Trump administration's April 2nd tariff announcements, as markets adjusted to the reality that AI funding would require companies to raise capital on an ongoing basis.

2 Massive capital spending on AI is creating opportunities and challenges

We believe the AI investment cycle is still in its early innings and may involve potential for shorter-term volatility.

3 We believe active management will be important in this environment

Strategic stock selection will be particularly crucial in navigating this market environment and the AI innovation wave, in our opinion.

Investment objective

The fund seeks long-term capital appreciation.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M) 17,493.44

Portfolio managers

Ido Cohen, Ronald Zibelli

Manager perspective and outlook

  • US equities saw renewed volatility in the fourth quarter amid economic crosscurrents and mixed investor sentiment toward artificial intelligence (AI) valuations and AI-related spending.
  • The longest government shutdown in US history further dampened confidence, as key economic data were delayed. Once released, the initial third-quarter GDP estimate showed robust growth of 4.3%. However, labor market conditions softened.
  • Consumer spending remained resilient but showed signs of strain among lower income households. Inflation moderated but stayed above the US Federal Reserve's (Fed) 2% target.
  • Against this backdrop, the Fed cut the federal funds rate twice during the quarter, lowering the target range to 3.50% - 3.75%.
  • We have a constructive outlook for US equities, supported by a resilient economy, strong corporate profits, reduced regulatory burdens, tax cuts and tax credits, as well as Fed interest rate cuts.
  • If 2025 was about building the AI foundation, we believe 2026 is poised to be about scaling and monetizing AI across the global economy.
  • Despite potential for short-term volatility, we believe AI will be one of the largest investment cycles of the last 100 years and we estimate this cycle is still in its early innings, particularly when viewed through the lens of forward growth.
  • As always, we remain agile and data-driven, ready to adjust fund positioning as conditions evolve.

Portfolio positioning

At quarter end, the fund's largest overweights were in industrials, energy and communication services. Within the industrials and energy sectors, AI-related energy demand continues to drive electrical infrastructure and power generation supply chain spending. The fund's industrials exposure also includes companies poised to benefit from higher global defense spending. We favor communication services for its AI-driven monetization potential. The largest underweight was health care, due to loss of Affordable Care Act subsidies and reduced health care insurance expected in 2026. Information technology (IT) is also an underweight due to substantial underweights in Apple and Microsoft, relative to their very large index weights. Financials, which should benefit from stimulus from tax cuts and bank deregulation, was close to equal weight.

New Positions

Argenx (ARGX) We sought to increase biotech exposure with what we consider the best large-cap option. We believe Argenx is catalyst rich over the next six months, with five phase III FDA readouts coming, and should also benefit from lower interest rates.

Caterpillar (CAT) Power is 25% of company equipment sales, which is ideal for the backup and bridge power applications data center hyperscalers need for the AI build-out. Mining is also an important market for the company and the recent bid for metals will be a tailwind for investment. Lower interest rates are another tailwind.

Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) This leading multinational electronic brokerage firm benefits from the tailwind of market volatility and increased use of digital tokens to represent real-world assets.

Notable Sales

KKR (KKR) and Blackstone (BX) Stocks of alternative asset managers like KKR and Blackstone have been struggling technically and have tended to decline when there are credit concerns, which is increasingly likely. Also, neither KKR nor Blackstone will benefit from growing use of digital tokens, which we see as an important theme for 2026.

Oracle (ORCL) Oracle may not see clarity on its partner investments for some time. Also, it has a much higher legacy debt load than other companies in the database solutions space. We rotated this capital to chip maker Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) following its strong analyst day.

Boston Scientific (BSX) Boston Scientific makes minimally invasive medical devices such as pacemakers, catheters and stents. We still like the company, but we thought the bulk of its growth was behind it for the time being and sold the position in favor of stocks where we had higher conviction.

Performance highlights

The fund (VAFAX) had a negative return for the quarter and underperformed its benchmark. Stock selection in financials, IT, health care and communication services were key detractors. An underweight in health care also hurt. Conversely, stock selection in industrials, energy and real estate, as well as an overweight in communication services, added to relative return.

Contributors to performance

Alphabet Alphabet will sell TPUs (tensor processing units) to Meta for its large-scale AI workloads, expanding Alphabet's total addressable market and diversifying Meta's reliance away from NVIDIA GPUs. Google also unveiled Nano Banana Pro, an image generation and editing model built on Gemini 3.

Lam Research designs semiconductor manufacturing equipment, enabling advanced chip production. Strong results stemmed from robust AI-driven chip demand and AI investments despite sector volatility.

Apple benefited from strong iPhone sales, growth in services like iCloud and Apple Music, and robust holiday demand for AI-powered devices.

Broadcom gains reflected strong quarterly earnings, robust AI chip demand and expanding software margins, which appeared to boost investor confidence despite sector volatility. Strategic partnerships and record free cash flow reinforced bullish sentiment.

Amazon.com beat earnings expectations with acceleration in AWS (Amazon Web Services) cloud growth. Amazon has also benefited from significant AI infrastructure investments and positive sentiment toward its investment in Anthropic (not a fund holding), which signaled its renewed competitiveness in cloud and AI markets.

Detractors from performance

Microsoft's stock weakness stemmed from slowing personal computer demand, higher AI infrastructure costs and cautious enterprise spending despite strong cloud adoption.

Meta Platforms shares fell after earnings showed a sharp profit drop from a one-time tax charge. Strong revenue was overshadowed by higher AI infrastructure spending, cautious 2026 expense guidance and Reality Labs losses tied to Quest VR and AI glasses research and development.

Coinbase Global stock was weak due to crypto trading volumes and a bitcoin selloff. We believe the long-term outlook remains positive given rising institutional interest, lighter regulation and stablecoin growth via Circle (not a fund holding) for merchant adoption.

Oracle saw slower cloud growth due to intense competition, delayed enterprise AI spending and slower migration of legacy workloads amid cautious IT budgets. We sold the stock during the quarter due to higher debt levels and uncertainty about partner investments.

Netflix and Paramount (not a fund holding) are in a high stakes bidding war, primarily for Warner Bros. Discovery (not a fund holding). Antitrust review could take 18 months, which means Netflix will likely be in limbo for the time being. We reduced the position.

data.title: U.S.-China Trade Falls Despite Being Propped Up By Soybean Exports
data.description:
The United States' top export to China is soybeans. That's why the news the U.S. and China had struck a trade deal at the end of October 2025 was so exciting, because it came with a commitment from China to buy 12 million metric tons of U.S.-grown soybeans.

China's soybean buyers completed their purchases before the end of 2025, the bulk of which were exported in December 2025 and January 2026. But instead of giving a large boost to trade between the two nations, that increase in exports only delivered a small increase over the November 2025 low, with the boost offset by the falling level of trade of other goods.

In February 2026, the boost from the negotiated soybean sales to China ended, and the combined value of goods exchanged between the U.S. and China fell to $26.9 billion. That level falls below the 2020 coronavirus pandemic low for this data series.

The following chart shows that negative data trend for the monthly value of trade between the U.S. and China.

We had anticipated a larger boost to that overall trade from China's soybean purchases from the U.S., but that impact has proven to be disappointing.

It's clear the level of trade won't return to its pre-2025 tariff war levels anytime soon. The trailing twelve-month average of that trade is $18.8 billion below our counterfactual projection of what the level of trade between the U.S. and China would look like in the absence of the trade war between them in February 2026.

That brings the decline in the trailing twelve-month average of the direct trade between the U.S. and China to a cumulative loss of $108.5 billion in the value of goods exchanged between the U.S. and China.

Meanwhile, reports indicate China is turning to alternatives to reduce its need to import large quantities of soybeans, which, if successful, will negatively impact soybean farmers in both the U.S. and Brazil, the largest exporters of soybeans to China:

At the edge of one of the many pig farms spread across the vast, unbroken floodplains of Taizhou, a two-hour drive northwest of ​Shanghai, a pair of square, four-metre pools of acrid-smelling ochre liquid hold the key to cutting costly soybean use in half.

The pools hold a swill of cheaper, locally sourced ingredients, which ‌can include brans, pumpkin vines and wine lees. But it is fermented - like yogurt - so the proteins are already broken down and easy to digest, lessening the need for the higher-quality proteins in soy, 80% of which China imports....

The government sharply accelerated a drive ​to expand protein sources for livestock in March of last year, just as trade tensions ramped up early into President Donald Trump's second term. Soybeans quickly became a ⁠key bargaining chip.

Reuters interviews with dozens of livestock and feed producers, state researchers and industry experts revealed Beijing is moving faster than previously thought to deploy new technologies and promote fermented feed.

It's the agricultural equivalent of Beijing's campaign to build ​domestic capabilities in microchips and artificial intelligence, catalysed by Washington's stringent controls on advanced technology exports to China.

A permanent loss of soybean exports would further hammer the level of trade between the U.S. and China.

References

U.S. Census Bureau. U.S. International Trade in Goods and Services (FT900). U.S. Trade in Goods with China, Not Seasonally Adjusted, Nominal Figures, Total Census Basis. [Online database]. Accessed 19 February 2026.

data.title: ADT: Business Needs To Show Earnings Growth
data.description:
Investment Overview

I wrote about ADT Inc. (ADT) previously with a hold rating, as the underlying fundamentals were not holding up well. For instance, customer retention was soft, dealer volumes were weak, and also the State Farm catalyst was not playing out as I expected. The slowdown I was worried about has now shown up in the reported numbers, and the FY2026 guide suggests the near-term earnings bridge is still messy. That said, I am not ready to turn bearish because ADT is still generating strong cash, holding healthy margins, and has enough financial flexibility to keep investing while returning capital to shareholders.

The Slowdown Happened as Expected

I had a cautious view on ADT in my previous update because the underlying operating metrics were not painting a picture of growth. While total revenue still grew 7% y/y to $1.287 billion, and adj. EBITDA grew 7%, churn had already moved up to 12.8% (20 bps sequential increase), and the pace of State Farm ramp was not great. Turns out, I was right. For those that missed the Q4 earnings numbers, here are some data for reference. Revenue growth slowed sharply, from 7% in Q2 2025 to just 1.3% y/y in Q4 2025. Security Installation and Other did continue to grow, but the pace of growth slowed from 44% in Q2 to just 10% in Q4.

The FY2026 guide did not help to ease concerns regarding this slowdown either. As a recap, management guided flat revenue and flat adj. EPS growth for FY26, but they also guided for churn to worsen to 13.1%, and the bridge to this is not great. Specifically, the multifamily divestiture reduced 4Q recurring monthly revenue [RMR] by $2.6 million, or ~$30 million on an annualized basis. On top of that, there are $50 million of added growth investments and a $45 million tariff headwind. As you can see, this is not one clean issue with one clean fix. It seems to me part of the drag is structural, part of it comes from management choosing to invest harder, and part of it is really external. There are just too many moving parts here that make the near-term earnings bridge not clean.

This is also why I think the medium-term targets are not getting much credit right now. After this rather horrendous quarter, and with management guiding flat revenue and flat adj. EPS growth for FY2026, investors are not going to anchor on the longer-term guide first. My expectation is they are going to ask whether the current business is good enough to support those long-term targets (5% revenue growth, ~10% adj. EPS growth, and 10% free cash flow growth). This is not entirely impossible, but as of where we are today, those targets are just not something the market will underwrite today.

Counterbalance View to Support a Neutral Rating

As to why I am not hasty to downgrade ADT to a sell is because this is still not a business with collapsing economics. This is still a business that prints $670 million adj. EBITDA in Q4 and has adj. EBITDA margin of 52.5%. It is also printing a good chunk of adj. free cash flow ($139 million). Of course, this is not to say fundamentals will improve, but this gives ADT the financial flexibility (or, aka, balance sheet strength) to invest heavily to steer topline growth back to positive. I would also say there is still a decent amount of stability in the business model, with ~85% of revenue coming from recurring monitoring subscriptions. Unless churn really accelerates to, say, 20 or 30%, ADT should continue to print a sizeable amount of cash. Also, remember that ADT has a choice to just return more capital to shareholders (which helps support the share price). They have, in fact, expanded the share repurchase program to $1.5 billion over the next 3 years after repurchasing ~$600 million in FY25 (no repurchases in 4Q25). ADT certainly has no problem servicing this remaining $900 million, which, if it pulls forward, could imply a yield of ~17% in 2026.

The other reason is Origin AI. ADT acquired Origin AI for $170 million in cash and expects to bring the technology into its offerings in 2027. I think the logic is fairly straightforward. If the technology works as intended, it should make the service more useful (better motion analytics), help ADT hold on to customers better (better retention), potentially open up some higher-tier upsell, and take some cost out of the system by cutting down on false alarms. ADT also signed a five-year agreement with Verisure worth $30 million to continue licensing the technology. I would not model the financial impact of this with high confidence for now since the contribution is still unclear and the timing is not near-term. Still, it gives ADT at least one credible product-led path to improve the model later, especially on retention, which is one of the issues today.

Valuation

I believe the reason why ADT is likely to continue trading at a discount to its peers (those offering security services like Brink's Co. (BCO), CoreCivic (CXW), and Alarm.com Holdings (ALRM)) is really because of the recent growth weakness and FY2026. My base view is that investors hate uncertainty, and this is a situation where one cannot aggressively "buy in" to the long-term view since FY2026 could be another year of growth deceleration. Just comparing the multiples relatively, ADT trades at 4.8x forward EBITDA, while BCO, CXW, and ALRM trade at ~6.3x, ~6.7x, and ~8x, and these peers all have a higher EBITDA growth outlook (ranging from mid-single-digits to as high as 20%) vs. ADT at basically flat. My view is that multiples will stay range-bound for a while until ADT shows signs of growth acceleration.

Conclusion

I maintain a hold rating on ADT. In my view, the most recent numbers confirmed that the underlying slowdown was real, and the FY2026 guide did little to rebuild confidence in the near-term growth outlook. The reason I do not go more negative is that ADT still has solid cash generation, a stable recurring revenue base, and some longer-term product upside from Origin AI. My bottom line is that the stock likely stays range-bound until ADT can show better subscriber trends, improved retention, and a cleaner path back to growth.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity Freedom® Funds (the Funds) are designed so that the target date referenced in the Fund name is the approximate year when investors expect to retire.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom® Retirement Fund (FFFAX), each of the Funds seeks high total return until reaching its respective target retirement date; thereafter, each Fund's objective will be to seek high current income and, as a secondary objective, capital appreciation.
  • Except for Fidelity Freedom® Retirement Fund, each Fund's asset allocation strategy becomes increasingly diversified as it approaches its target date – and beyond. Ultimately, the Funds are expected to merge with Fidelity Freedom Retirement Fund.
  • The Funds employ a disciplined and time-tested investment process focused on helping investors achieve successful retirement outcomes by leveraging the depth and strength of Fidelity's investment research and resources.

Performance Attribution Summary

  • Active asset allocation positioning and security selection added value to the fund's performance versus the Composite index in Q4. The fund's overweight in non-U.S. equities meaningfully contributed, as investments in both developed-markets equities and emerging-markets equities outperformed U.S. equities during the quarter.
  • The impact of active security selection varied by asset class. The strong performance of U.S. large-cap managers resulted in a relative contribution from U.S. equities, whereas non-U.S. managers, in aggregate, underperformed the Composite index.

Relative Contributors (representative across vintages):

Large-cap U.S. equity funds stood out to the upside this quarter, including the following notable relative contributors:

  • Fidelity® Series Growth Company Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 3000® Growth Index. The fund is managed by Steve Wymer, who invests across a spectrum of companies, from blue-chip growth to aggressive growth. Steve focuses on firms operating in well-positioned industries and niches, and those he considers capable of delivering persistent sales and earnings growth. Security selection within the information technology sector contributed to the fund's relative result this quarter, especially out-of-benchmark positions in Sandisk and Ciena, from the technology hardware & equipment industry. A non-benchmark position in private company Space Exploration Technologies, a maker of spacecraft and provider of satellite communications and launch capabilities, also notably contributed.
  • Fidelity® Series Large Cap Stock Fund outperformed its benchmark, the S&P 500® index. The fund, managed by Matt Fruhan, emphasizes companies for which he believes long-term normalized earnings and free cash flow are underappreciated by investors. Security selection contributed to the fund's relative result, notably within the industrials and financials sectors. Within financials, overweight positions in Wells Fargo and Bank of America added relative value, while larger-than-benchmark stakes in GE Vernova and United Parcel Service stood out in the industrials sector.

Relative Detractor (representative across vintages):

The largest fund-level detractor:

  • Fidelity® Series Overseas Fund underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index. The fund is managed by Vincent Montemaggiore, who is valuation sensitive and focuses on higher-quality companies with durable growth. Security selection detracted this quarter, particularly within the industrials sector. Within the sector, an overweight in Thales, a French company that provides advanced technologies, services and products to the defense, aerospace, space and cyber markets, hurt the fund's relative result most. An out-of-benchmark position in Baycurrent, one of Japan's largest consulting firms, also notably detracted, as did an overweight stake in Japanese food and biotechnology company Ajinomoto.

Active Asset Allocation:

  • The fund outperformed the Composite index for the quarter, driven by active asset allocation positioning.

Outlook and Positioning

  • Target-date funds are long-term strategies designed to help keep participants on track toward their retirement goals through different market environments. Strategic asset allocation decisions are focused on providing age-appropriate asset allocation throughout participants' changing time horizons.
  • Our research highlights several macro-oriented themes that we believe underscore the need for portfolio diversification. Government debt, global fragmentation, aging demographics and new technology are likely to impact long-term growth and inflation. We believe diversification in fixed income and inflation-resistant assets remains attractive to hedge these risks.
  • In 2025, we announced an update to the glide path and strategic asset allocation of the strategies, based on our long-term views of participant behavior and our capital markets research. We increased exposure to equities for early-career investors and to equities and inflation-sensitive assets for investors near and in retirement. Implementation of the updated allocations is underway, and we expect to complete the transition within the next six to 12 months.
  • Active asset allocation positioning relative to the strategic benchmark reflects our intermediate-term views on assets that are mispriced relative to our estimate of fair value. Active risk in the funds is at the low end of the historical range, based on a view that elevated asset valuations, particularly for U.S. stocks, provide little cushion against near-term economic and geopolitical risk. We believe valuations outside of the AI epicenter are reasonable, and many sectors have improving growth expectations.
  • As of the end of 2025, the funds were overweight equities, albeit by a smaller magnitude since the start of the quarter. We maintain a preference for non-U.S. assets relative to U.S. assets, based on attractive valuations and the potential for non-U.S. assets to benefit from continued U.S. dollar weakness.
  • We added to fixed-income investments in the fourth quarter, and we view intermediate-term U.S. Treasuries as the most attractive part of the Treasury yield curve. The Funds remain underweight investment-grade bonds, as spreads are low relative to history.
  • Inflation remains sticky and upside risk may be underappreciated by investors. Supply-side indicators, including housing and labor markets, pressure the intermediate-term horizon, in our view. As a result, in Q4 we added to the funds' overweight in commodities.

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: Moody's: Big Buybacks Are Creating A Floor Price
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Have you ever met someone who was extremely nice and pleasant to be around but was ultimately extremely forgettable? My wife and I had an acquaintance in college whom we enjoyed spending time with, but incidentally, we routinely failed to think of them when we were making plans. We didn't have anything against them, and while we were not exceptionally close friends with them, we would spend time with them occasionally. They were someone who just seemed to fall through the cracks within our social circle.

When it comes to investing, investors often struggle with this same problem. You see, we have companies we hear about regularly, and we just assume that because everyone is aware of them, they're going to be extremely overvalued or perhaps extremely unattractive as an investment at all. We would assume that because they're so well known, they will be extremely crowded companies, and the value is just not going to be there to be attractive. However, I have found that there are some companies that simply slip through the cracks of investors' minds because of those reasons, and the value remains attractive even though they're exceptionally well-known.

Likely, when it comes to a company's credit rating, you ask yourself what the credit rating is by Moody's or S&P Global. Yet do you invest in those companies that have such a massive, well-known reach, or do you look right past them?

A Legendary Business With a Tailwind Trifecta

Moody's Corporation (MCO) is a multifaceted business with multiple well-known segments. Readers are probably most familiar with its Moody's Investors Service or MIS segment, which forms a global duopoly alongside S&P Global (SPGI). Any time corporate debt is refinanced or new debt is raised, a fee is charged by these rating agencies. That comprised approximately half (53.4%) of the company's $7.72 billion in total 2025 revenue.

Then, there's the Moody's Analytics or MA segment. This is a subscription-based (SaaS) business that provides financial intelligence and analytical tools. That made up the remaining 46.6% of total revenue in 2025, with 95%+ of its revenue being recurring.

Moody's closed out 2025 with notable strength. The company's total revenue rose by 13% year-over-year to $1.89 billion in Q4 2025. This topped the analyst consensus for the quarter by $19 million.

That was made possible by a 16.9% uptick in MIS revenue to $946 million during the fourth quarter. This was powered by a surge in issuances from hyperscalers who tapped markets to fund AI capex and private credit transaction revenue momentum (+40% in Q4).

The MA segment reported $943 million in revenue in the fourth quarter, which was up 9.3% over the year-ago period. That was driven by continued AI adoption (Moody's GenAI solutions grew at twice the rate of the broader customer base, which demonstrates its value).

Moody's adjusted diluted EPS surged 38.9% higher year-over-year to $3.64 for the fourth quarter. This exceeded the analyst consensus during the quarter by $0.20. That was fueled by operating leverage in MIS (the adjusted operating margin expanded by 720 basis points to 58.5% in Q4). The scalability of MA was another factor, with the incremental cost of adding a new subscriber being minimal (the adjusted operating margin improved by 190 basis points to 35.7% in Q4).

Looking ahead, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted diluted EPS to compound by 12.3% annually through 2028, off a 2025 base of $14.94. Moody's is also a financially healthy company, sporting an A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

Moody's 0.9% dividend yield comes in below the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 1.2% yield. The dividend is quite secure, with the payout ratio likely to be in the mid-20% range for 2026. That should enable Moody's to keep handing out approximately 10% annual dividend growth (for a Chowder number of 11) to extend its 17-year dividend growth streak.

While Moody's is not extremely undervalued, it's still a solid value in our view. At the current $444 share price (as of April 7th, 2026), shares are priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 26.5. This is meaningfully less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 28.8, according to FAST Graphs.

Looking ahead, we think that a fair value P/E ratio of 28 is reasonable for Moody's. That's because its 12.3% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth consensus demonstrates its fundamentals to be intact versus the 10-year CAGR of 12.5%.

Since we always prefer a margin of safety, we adjusted the fair value multiple one turn lower. This signals that even with a slight cushion, Moody's stock is trading at a 10% discount to our $482 fair value per share estimate.

If it lives up to growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could deliver a 20% total return through December 2027. By the end of 2031, Moody's would be primed for 12% annual total returns.

Of course, there are risks to the company's investment thesis.

One of the most notable risks to Moody's currently is the fact that it has aggressively moved into rating private credit and asset-backed finance. On the one hand, it's enabling strong business growth. On the other hand, it comes with reputational risk similar to the Great Recession if a widespread downturn hits the sector and its models don't properly account for the connection between private lenders and traditional banks.

A long-term risk is the possibility that AI becomes so efficient at credit analysis that the value premium charged for its ratings erodes. If proprietary models become commoditized, this could result in margin compression for Moody's. That could cause its valuation multiple to re-rate lower.

Conclusion

Moody's is not only an attractive company from a long-term growth viewpoint but also from their ability to continue to reward shareholders with their dividend growth. Moody's management is not blind to the fact that its share price has fallen in 2026. They've indicated, even in public conferences, that they have been heavily buying back shares from the beginning of this year and increased their share buyback plan by 25% from last year. This is a big boost to try and bring a bottom to their share price and reward current shareholders by removing those who want to exit. Shares that are bought back and retired help provide more value to every share that remains and provide more room for dividend growth because the overall cost of their dividend goes down with each share that they purchased.

Investing in companies that are well-known and hold dominant market positions can be highly rewarding, especially if you're willing to buy them only when they are fairly valued or undervalued. I don't believe in blindly buying a company just because it is well-known. I believe in buying exceptionally high-quality companies at fair or undervalued prices and enjoying the rewards of that position. That takes time, effort, and, most of all, clarity.

A Dividend King enjoys a position of clarity by rising above the noise.

data.title: EPR Properties: AI And Streaming Fears Overblown While Diversifying Away From Theaters
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered EPR Properties (EPR), I highlighted their robust AFFO growth, solid fundamentals, and well-covered, attractive monthly dividend as they are undergoing a significant portfolio transformation.

Following a solid year, ramped-up portfolio transformation, and a more attractive valuation and yield, EPR is upgraded to Strong Buy despite potential for near-term macro pressure, trading at a significant discount to its fair value.

Portfolio Transformation Ramping Up

EPR reported a solid 2025, with Q4 FFO being in line with the market's estimates alongside a beat on revenue, with the AFFO growing by ~7% to $398.2 million (slightly above my estimate of $395.80 million), while the AFFO/share also grew by a very solid ~6% compared to 2024.

The year came with significant portfolio rebalancing activities, expanding their golf portfolio alongside other experiential properties in Q4 and announcing a massive $168.3 million in dispositions in 2025, selling 33 theaters over the past 5 years and currently having 1 more vacant theater left, aiming at reducing this theater exposure further alongside their small education portfolio.

The company continued this expansion beyond Q4, recently announcing a deal for a portfolio of 7 regional parks in a $342 million transaction from Six Flags Entertainment (FUN), marking their largest acquisition since 2017 and further expanding their portfolio away from theaters - with the acquisition of 6 parks already closed and 1 more expected to close in Q2.

For 2026, they expect this to accelerate, with guidance for $400 million to $500 million in investment spending (as previously expected) and $25 million to $75 million in disposition proceeds throughout the year, while the FFO, as adjusted per share is expected to reach $5.28 to $5.48, meaning an increase of 5.08% YoY.

Financially, based on EPR's latest report, we continue to see a relatively normal position for a REIT, with debt that's covered by their real estate properties well, with their net debt to annualized adjusted EBITDAre standing at 4.9x as of the end of the year, below the lower end of their target range.

Meanwhile, although they have $1 billion undrawn on their revolving credit facility and all debt is fixed through interest rate swaps at a 4.4% weighted average interest rate, the current environment may push them into more expensive refinancing, which can add more pressure in the form of interest expenses. The company's CFO also highlighted the following during their Q4 Earnings Call:

In November, we closed on $550 million of new 5-year senior unsecured notes at a coupon of 4.75%. And at year-end, we had $90.6 million of cash on hand and no balance drawn on our $1 billion revolver. Additionally, in December, we finalized our new ATM program. While no equity issuance is required to fund our plan for 2026, given that we project to be below the midpoint of our target leverage range at year-end without any such issuance.

Following their crash and the 5.1% increase in their monthly dividend, EPR now stands at a yield of about 7.3% at the moment of writing this, representing a very solid payout ratio of about 72.37% based on their 2025 AFFO, or even better if the AFFO grows.

I'll also mention the company's 3 preferred stocks since some of them are worth considering, in my opinion:

First of all, the redeemable perpetual preferred EPR.PR.G (5.75%, $25 liquidation preference) is a solid option, as despite being callable since November 2022, it should be safe for now given the fact that the company's common stock actually comes at a higher yield than the ~7.15% they're currently trading at, and it certainly looks more attractive now given the price of $20.14 it's trading at. Given the small base yield, I don't see this as something they'd call any time soon, as the cost of capital (debt and equity) is likely higher than this 5.75%, and even if they decide to call it for some reason, we're still talking about a ~24% profit, which is not bad at all.

There's also EPR.PR.E (with a 9% base) and EPR.PR.C (5.75% base), both of which being convertible perpetual preferreds issued back during the boom and subsequent bust back in 2006 and 2008, highlighting what issuing in a good and terrible environment looks like. They currently pay a 7.32% and 6.24% yield, respectively, and E continues to be the most attractive one now, in my opinion. That's because we are talking about an instrument that can't be forced into conversion unless the price appreciates 150% based on the applicable conversion price from back then, meaning about $77.40 at December 31, 2025 for Series E, which would imply prices similar to the levels from back before 2020, although I continue to believe that the common is better for my risk-reward appetite.

EPR should release their next earnings report on April 29th, with the market expecting FFO of $1.24 per share on 179.33 million in revenue. Given the ongoing portfolio moves and transition away from movie theaters, I expect them to continue this portfolio transformation for now, although I wouldn't be surprised to see an overall negative tone regarding the broader environment due to the ongoing Iran conflict.

Macro Uncertainty Rising

Right now, it's no surprise to see the stock's weak performance during March, as the Iran conflict can have significant implications on the economy and discretionary budgets, which would directly affect the company's tenants.

Not only are we talking about significant jumps in pump prices and potential for inflation in virtually everything that can last for longer than the market may initially believe due to the damage done by Iran's retaliatory attacks on their neighbors' energy infrastructure, but this inflationary shock can affect the Fed's next moves.

We can see treasury yields already jumping as a result of the market's initial response being a flight back to the dollar, and we already know very well what a higher-for-longer rate environment or even hikes can do to the valuation of most REITs, as these companies depend on the cost of external capital since they have to pay most of what they make back to shareholders.

With Trump's recent threat that "a whole civilization will die tonight," while others consider that the markets are "completely wrong" regarding the Iran conflict and believe that oil could even reach $200/bbl, it's hard to see a de-escalation happen overnight, although I still believe (and hope) that we're likely to see an end to this relatively soon given the economic, political, religious, military, etc. implications and casualties of a prolonged war.

However, that's not to say that a de-escalation would solve this overnight. In fact, we're very likely getting to a point where even a complete de-escalation today would still lead to major inflationary pressure in the near future, pushing central banks into rethinking their trajectory of rate reductions as they brace for the impact, with rising uncertainty surrounding the Fed's next moves while the ECB chief already stated that they're ready to hike as soon as possible.

EPR's Future?

Over the past years, we've had worries surrounding the movie theater industry's future due to the ongoing "streaming wars" and even AI disruption risk that fellow analyst Jussi Askola mentioned in his recent article, potentially leading to a change in the content production/distribution landscape.

Although this is a real risk, and finding another usage for a movie theater is very tough since we're talking about a very specific and specialized segment of real estate, it can still take several years (more like decades) until this would hurt the existing properties, in my opinion, as we're still talking about going to the movies to have an experience rather than just watching content. People had access to these movies well before AI or streaming services became popular, yet we still went to the cinema.

On the other hand, AI can boost the industry and result in more content available in cinema, which can be a counterargument to the expected impact. Note that I'm not talking about random AI content, but rather professionals using it to save significant time or cut budgets for otherwise expensive stunts, directive/script issues, etc., which can even be bullish for EPR's properties.

Yes, these are risks that come on top of the macro pressures that can fundamentally hurt REITs, but until/if we see them affecting the market (which can initially respond through less demand for new properties before being hurt), we're talking about properties that still generate solid amounts of money and can still be sold in the foreseeable future while EPR is advancing its diversification and ramping up their investments.

Regardless, it's very unlikely to see these hurt the company significantly by the time they achieve their diversification goals, which EPR's CEO also highlighted, stating that they plan to reduce this exposure to even less than 20% over the next 3 to 5 years, replying with the following when asked if this would be achieved through selling or adding new properties during Citi's Miami Global Property CEO Conference from March 2026:

I mean we've sold 33 theaters over the last year -- over the last 1.5 years [AUTHOR'S CORRECTION: this is a transcript/audio mistake; it's actually 5 years]. So we've done -- we're doing both, and we'll continue to look and do both. So again, it's not -- if there's an opportunity to sell and accelerate that, we'll take a look at that. So it's really about pulling both levers.

Risks Reiterated

Although the company might be mostly "out" of the theater industry by the time these specific risks for the industry would affect them, selling properties during such an environment can be terrible due to the Iran conflict and its inflationary pressure causing significant uncertainty and potential rate hikes or a higher-for-longer rate environment, at the very least.

Meanwhile, given their relatively elevated level of debt that's due over the next half a decade and will have to be refinanced year after year, it wouldn't be impossible to see higher interest expenses as a result, while issuing stock is also very expensive and hard to beat through regular real estate investments.

Valuation

For EPR's DCF to Equity, starting from their 2025 performance and the guidance, we should likely see an AFFO of about $418.11 million in 2026, which I'll assume can grow at a 4% CAGR (slightly below the market's estimates) despite their significant investments and potential for lower refinancing rates in the future, with a relatively conservative 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a 10% discount rate to take into account their volatility and specific risks, with 80.685 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $5.94 billion, for an intrinsic value of $73.67 per share, which continues to be well above the current $52.05.

Conclusion

EPR is upgraded to Strong Buy, trading at an attractive valuation and sustainable yield while standing to benefit from their portfolio transition and potential macro improvements in the longer term.

There are risks to consider, and there's room for this to go down more if the environment deteriorates and the market has to price in more pressure, but that's going to be the case for most REITs and stocks out there.

data.title: Caterpillar: AI-Driven Power Demand And Strong Backlog Support Continued Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered Caterpillar (CAT) in December 2025, and the stock is up a good ~20% since then versus a slight decline in S&P 500. I expect this outperformance to continue moving forward. Caterpillar is poised to see good revenue growth given its strong backlog, dealer destocking ending and secular demand from AI-driven data center and power infrastructure work. In the medium term a potential cyclical recovery in construction and mining business should also support revenue growth. The revenue growth should also benefit from price increases and expanding services exposure.

On the margin front, the company should benefit from operating leverage on volume growth. Further, pricing discipline and contractual pricing escalators should help to offset cost headwinds.

While the stock is trading at a premium to its historical levels, the stronger earnings growth profile justify a premium. According to consensus expectations, Caterpillar is expected to deliver strong double-digit EPS growth (~20% annual EPS growth) for the next few years. I believe the stock still offers good upside potential from current levels. Hence, I rate it a buy.

Recent Financial Performance

CAT’s net sales were up 18% Y/Y in Q425 driven by higher sales volumes across all segments. The price realization was neutral in the quarter.

Segment-wise, the Construction Industries segment’s sales benefited from higher sales volume across North America. In the Resource Industries segment, sales growth was driven by higher sales volumes and favorable changes in dealer inventories. In the Power & Energy segment, sales benefited from higher price realization, increased volumes, and strong demand across oil and gas and power generation end markets.

The company’s adjusted operating margin declined by 270 bps Y/Y to 18.3%, largely due to $1.03 bn in higher manufacturing costs from tariffs. On a segment basis, adjusted operating margin declined in Construction Industries and Resource Industries, while the Power & Energy segments saw slight improvement.

Outlook

Looking forward, I am optimistic about the company’s revenue growth prospects. Caterpillar exited 2025 with a strong backlog of $51 bn, up 71% Y/Y and $11 bn sequentially. The strong backlog provides a good visibility for future revenue growth. The strong demand environment is also helping the company to pass through tariff-related and other cost inflation. The management expects 2% price realization in 2026. For long-term contracts, the company has used escalation clauses to protect against input cost volatility. So, the pricing should be a good growth driver.

The dealer inventory destocking, which was a headwind last year, should abate. Dealer inventory declined by $500 mn during 2025 as dealers chose to reduce their inventories due to tariff-related macro uncertainty. However, contrary to their perception, the demand has stayed strong. I am not expecting any further destocking and the dealers to return to normalized practices in 2026. Considering the strong demand environment, dealers can start restocking as well which should support revenue growth.

In the Construction Industries segment, the company is benefiting from data center buildouts. Further, non-residential construction is seeing good demand supported by reshoring and IIJA funding. If we look at the pipeline of data centre and reshoring megaprojects in the U.S., this theme is expected to continue driving growth for the next several years. Also, while the recent Iran war has raised some concerns about inflation and further rate cuts this year, I believe the long-term trend for the interest rate is still lower. So, residential and light commercial activity should also improve in the medium to long run as interest rates decline.

Further, the management noted that China is showing early signs of recovery in above 10-ton excavator category and the Europe is stabilizing. Through Caterpillar financial, the company is offering interest rate buydown to support small equipment buyers which helped it gain share in 2025, and these share gains should continue moving forward.

In the Resource Industries segment, rising commodity prices should help revenue growth further. Heavy construction, quarry and aggregate activity remains solid. Mining fleet utilization is also elevated and average fleet age remain high which should drive new equipment and replacement demand. In addition, the company’s autonomous hauling solutions is another secular growth driver. There were 827 autonomous medium and heavy duty trucks in operation at year end 2025, up from 690 at year end 2024. Recent agreements like mixed-fleet deployments in Brazil should further support growth.

In the Power & Energy segment, the company should beneﬁt from good demand for its power solutions driven by data centres buildouts and increasing AI adoption. The company is getting large scale orders including multi gigawatt prime power agreements. Further, the company is seeing strong demand for reciprocating engines for applications upto 10 MW and solar turbines. Management is increasing its capacity to meet this demand and its engine capacity is expected to double and gas turbine capacity should also increase through 2030.

Beyond equipment sales, management is targeting services revenue of $30 bn by 2030, up from $24 bn in 2025. The shift toward power applications with high run-time intensity supports long-term services and parts demand.

Overall, I am optimistic on CAT’s revenue growth prospects supported by strong backlog, dealer inventory restocking, strong demand in power generation, healthy commodity prices, improving rate environment and price realization and increasing services exposure. I am also optimistic about the company’s margin growth prospects. The margins should benefit from operating leverage as the volume outlook is positive.

Further, price increases should also help margins. Management expects approximately 2% price realization in 2026, which should help offset cost headwinds. Given strong demand in the company’s end markets, the company should be able to continue raising prices while maintaining its competitive position. This pricing discipline combined with operating leverage should help the company to offset cost headwinds.

The company is also incorporating inﬂationary cost pressures and price escalation clauses in its long term contracts which should protect against input cost volatility. Further, the company is also optimizing manufacturing footprint and supply chain, which should improve efficiency over time. Overall, the company’s margin outlook remain positive supported by operating leverage, pricing discipline, contractual protection mechanism and sourcing optimization initiatives.

Valuation

Caterpillar is currently trading at 31.62x FY26 consensus EPS of $22.88 and 25.99x FY27 consensus EPS of $27.75, which is a premium relative to its 5-year historical average P/E of 18.69x.

I believe this premium is justified given the company’s improved growth profile. Caterpillar is well-positioned to capitalize on rapid growth of AI data centres and related power infrastructure which is driving demand for prime power solutions. Prime power applications generates higher lifetime services revenue compared to traditional standby solutions, which should drive long term margin growth.

Further, with rates eventually turning lower in the medium term, residential and light commercial construction should recover. This, coupled with strong infrastructure spending and higher commodity prices (supporting mining capex and replacement cycle) bodes well for the company’s growth in the longer run.

One interesting thing is that companies with significant direct exposure to AI-driven power demand, such as GE Vernova (GEV), continue to trade at higher forward multiple, around 60x. Since investors are assigning premium valuations to power infrastructure companies benefiting from AI expansion, CAT’s 26x - 32x forward multiple appears reasonable.

There is also a potential for earnings upside. The company’s backlog increased 71% Y/Y, which should support revenue growth. However, consensus is modelling high single digit growth in the near term. Given the strong demand environment, pricing discipline, operating leverage and expanding services revenues, I believe Caterpillar can beat estimates and post better expected revenue and earnings growth in the coming quarters/years.

I believe the risk-reward is attractive given the strong growth prospects driven by secular prime power demand, cyclical recovery in mining, improving earnings quality, and strong backlog.

Risks

There are execution risks related to the company’s capacity expansion plans. Any delays in facility ramp-up or cost overruns could impact margins.

My thesis anticipates that declining interest rates should support a recovery in residential and light commercial construction. However, if interest rate cuts are delayed or smaller than expected, the recovery in construction activity may not happen and could impact volume growth.

Another risk is commodity price volatility. Any decline in commodity prices could lower capital spending, delay equipment replacement cycles and impact the company’s Resource Industries segment.

Takeaway

I believe Caterpillar has strong growth potential. The company should benefit from secular AI-driven power demand and cyclical recovery across construction and mining end markets, while the stock trades at a premium to historical averages, improving earnings quality, strong backlog, and services mix improvement justifies the premium.

data.title: U.S. And Iran Agree To Ceasefire
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

Chip fab: Intel (INTC) joins the Terafab project with SpaceX (SPACE) and Tesla (TSLA) to build chips to power AI and robotics.

No respite: The U.S. rejects Ford's (F) request seeking aluminum tariff relief after the Novelis fire caused supply bottlenecks.

Fire incident: A massive fire engulfs Kimberly-Clark's (KMB) 1.2M square foot paper goods warehouse in Southern California.

Pursuing peace

The U.S. announced a two-week ceasefire, suspending its attacks on Iran in exchange for Tehran reopening the Strait of Hormuz. The news sent U.S. stock index up about 3%, while Asian and European equities also rose. Oil prices (CL1:COM) (CO1:COM) plunged below $100/bbl as fears of prolonged supply disruption eased.

Major breakthrough: "I agree to suspend the bombing and attack of Iran for a period of two weeks," President Donald Trump posted on Truth Social, saying the ceasefire is subject to Iran agreeing to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. "We received a 10-point proposal from Iran and believe it is a workable basis on which to negotiate." Iran's 10-point plan includes demands for its continued control of the Strait of Hormuz, lifting of all sanctions, acceptance of its nuclear program and withdrawal of U.S. forces from the region. Pakistan, the main intermediary in the negotiations, invited the U.S. and Iran to Islamabad for further talks this Friday. To note, Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said the ceasefire included Lebanon, although Israel said it did not.

Reopening Hormuz: Iran and Oman are reportedly planning to charge fees from ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz during the ceasefire. Iran will use the fees it collects for reconstruction efforts, while Oman's plans for the funds remain unclear, Iran's semi-official Tasnim News Agency reported. "For two weeks, safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz will be possible via coordination with Iran's Armed Forces and with due consideration of technical limitations," Iran's Araghchi said. Meanwhile, Trump said the U.S. will help with the traffic buildup in the Strait of Hormuz. "Big money will be made. Iran can start the reconstruction process. We'll be loading up with supplies of all kinds, and just 'hangin' around' in order to make sure that everything goes well," he posted on Truth Social.

Supply outlook: While oil prices eased significantly, analysts say it will take time for supplies to normalize. "Further price direction will hinge on whether talks translate into a durable agreement and a sustained normalization of flows through the strait, with volatility likely to persist during negotiations later this week," said ING Economic and Financial Analysis. According to Kpler data, 172 Mbbl of crude and products are still on the water in the Gulf, spread across nearly 187 laden tankers. "While the ceasefire creates a window for transit, flows remain conditional and operationally constrained," Kpler said. Willie Walsh, director ​general of the International Air Transport Association, warned that jet fuel costs may stay elevated as "it will still take months to get back to where supply needs to be."

data.title: Applied Industrial: Growth Is Not Enticing Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been over two years since the first time I covered Applied Industrial Technologies, Inc. (AIT), so I thought I’d go through its performance over the last year because the company recently released its full-year 2025 numbers, too.

The company has been chugging along as expected, but unfortunately, the growth and margin expansion are not good enough to warrant a buy right now.

Performance

Starting from the top, AIT’s revenues showed a decent uptrend over the last year. It may not have been particularly rapid growth, but it is better than a downtrend. Looking at the segments in more detail, AIT’s Service Center-Based distribution segment was slightly pressured due to softer industrial activity, with organic growth coming in around 3% in the latest quarter. It is a growth, but it is quite weak indeed. Regarding its other revenue segment, the Engineered Solutions, this segment saw growth of around 19%, but most of that was due to the acquisition impact. Orders were up around 10% in the segment, which tells me there is demand. So, most of the total growth did come from the acquisitions, which kept AIT’s top-line performance intact, but I would prefer to see some decent organic growth.

On to the company’s profitability and efficiency, we can see a mixed bag of results over 2025. Gross margins remained steady, but the EBIT and the bottom line are down slightly in the second half of 2025. The company saw higher LIFO expenses during the period, which brought down its profitability slightly, as well as softer December sales. Q2 ’26 GAAP EPS came in at $2.51, which was a beat of 2 cents.

Looking at other important metrics in efficiency and profitability, the management is doing a good job of utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder equity quite efficiently, thus creating value. Additionally, it appears that the company also has a decent moat and pricing power since its return on total cost is well above the minimum I’d like to see of 10%.

Now, let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. AIT’s cash position was around $406m, while having around $572m in long-term debt. I don’t think this is a bad position at all. The company is quite profitable and can easily cover the interest expenses on the debt outstanding. The company barely pays anything on that debt. The latest figure was around $942K, while the company made $123 million in operating income, giving AIT a very solid interest coverage ratio of around 130x. A formidable balance sheet that can easily take up even more debt to further the growth.

Upcoming Quarter

The management tightened FY26’s EPS and sales guidance to $10.45-$10.75 from $10.10-$10.85 and 5.5%-7% from 4%-7%, respectively. EBITDA margin will be essentially flat at 12.2%-12.4%. So, the company’s updated guidance was more or less in line with what the expectations were from analysts.

Overall, the company seems to have been doing relatively well over the last year. I don’t see much improvement, but the company’s performance isn’t going down either. Just chugging along as it had in the past. I would have liked to see more operational improvements.

Service Center—Core MRO as key growth

The management sees the service center segment as a steady, MRO-centric grower. This growth is going to come from price, share gains, and tuck-ins, according to the management. I read this as the service center performance will be steady, but nothing exciting. Something along the lines of low single-digit organic growth, and maybe a bit more, depending on what those bolt-on and tuck-in acquisitions bring later over time. These are usually smaller in nature but still quite accretive to the overall performance.

This segment is built around selling and maintenance/repair parts and services, like bearings, power transmission, and fluid power MRO. This business does seem more reliable since maintenance and repair can be a bit more predictable. The spending is recurring and tied to keeping equipment running, so I think this will be quite resilient in any macro environment.

For now, since volumes were flat, the management pointed to the growth coming primarily from price increases. This could help the company’s operational efficiency, but you can’t raise prices indefinitely. The management also said that they see decent demand in technical MRO and highlighted some internal sales initiatives that will be the key drivers for 2026.

So, I am comfortable with around 3-4% growth in the long run, and anything above that, I would consider a massive success.

Engineered Solutions

This segment will be a lot more exciting, but it will also come with a bit more risk. Analysts and the management alike see this segment as the primary growth driver, with particular highlights within the Automation and advanced technology segment. In Q2, automation orders were up around 20%, so this contributed significantly to the overall orders being up 10% y/y. Customers seem to be feeling the need to automate more of their plants going forward, and so that is very positive for AIT, since they are sitting in the perfect spot that is comprised of components and engineering support. With labor prices continuing to climb, many firms are now turning to robotics investments to protect their margins in the long run. Additionally, automation reduces injury risks and improves consistency. These two are very critical for regulated industries.

In my opinion, the company will see some decent growth from data centers, semiconductors, and other verticals. There is still so much demand from data center hyperscalers that, frankly, I think it would be impossible not to make money if your operations are involved in any way, shape, or form in data center infrastructure and AI. The hyperscalers will be spending over $700 billion in capex this year alone, and I don’t see how this is going to slow down next year unless these enterprises suddenly calculate that all the investments they already made are not worth it. I don’t see that happening in 2027, because all of these capex dollars won’t yield anything for at least a few years, and then maybe they will be like, “That's enough,” and begin to wind down the spending spree and cut off the free money.

Closing Comments

The company would need to do a lot better in terms of the top-line growth and margin improvements before I would consider buying the company at these prices. I will be paying attention to how the following couple of quarters come in and what the management is going to say about the demand overall regarding the company’s products. I think we will see some growth acceleration because of the massive demand surge in data centers and AI, but I would like to see how well the management is going to capitalize on it and the robotics sector.
